summary:
The Jiroft civilisation, centred on the Halil Rud river valley in modern Kerman province in southeastern Iran, flourished approximately 2700–2200 BC and is known primarily from a remarkable corpus of chlorite (steatite) decorated vessels and artefacts that were first identified through illegal excavations and appeared on the international antiquities market in the late 1990s and early 2000s. Subsequent legal excavations by Iranian archaeologists at Konar Sandal and other sites in the Jiroft region have confirmed the existence of a substantial Bronze Age culture with monumental architecture, distinctive artistic traditions, and widespread commercial connections.

Jiroft chlorite vessels are decorated with elaborate mythological scenes featuring composite creatures, heroes, and architectural motifs that find close parallels in Mesopotamian and Elamite art of the same period. These objects have been found not only in the Jiroft region but also at sites across Mesopotamia, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Indus Valley, attesting to a wide trade network. Some scholars have proposed that Jiroft may be the ancient Aratta — a land mentioned in Sumerian texts as a distant and wealthy city providing lapis lazuli and other luxury goods — though this identification remains unproven.

The Jiroft civilisation expanded scholarly understanding of Bronze Age cultural complexity in Iran and demonstrated that the region between Mesopotamia and the Indus Valley was not an empty corridor but home to distinct civilisations participating actively in the interconnected world of the third millennium BC. The decipherment of any local script from this region, if found, would transform understanding of early Bronze Age Iran.
